sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 30-year-old female, presents with anxiety about recent blood work showing a positive hepatitis C antibody test.
She reports being surprised by this result as she has been feeling fine and was not previously aware of having hepatitis C.
She mentions a history of past IV drug use, which she stopped 15 years ago, and occasional heavy drinking.
She also reports smoking one to two cigarettes per day and feeling tired at the end of the day despite getting enough sleep.
She has a family history of hypertension, diabetes, and depression.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of IV drug use, which she stopped 15 years ago.
She also reports a history of heavy drinking, with a current intake of one beer per day.
She smokes one to two cigarettes per day.
Review of systems: The patient reports feeling tired at the end of the day, but otherwise feels good.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient appears in no distress and no jaundice on skin exam.
The heart exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm with a 2/6 systolic murmur, which has not changed from last year.
The lungs are clear with no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
The abdomen is soft with present bowel sounds and no hepatosplenomegaly.
Test Results: The patient's HCV antibody test is positive, and her liver panel shows an elevated AST at 39, but normal albumin and total bilirubin levels.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is diagnosed with hepatitis C, a viral infection that affects the liver, likely acquired years ago.
Problem list: Hepatitis C; Suspected liver disease; Suspected liver fibrosis.
Plan:
I prescribed hepatitis C treatment and ordered additional blood work to confirm the diagnosis, including HCV RNA and genotype testing.
I also ordered a special ultrasound to determine the severity of the patient's liver disease.
I will see the patient in three weeks to review the results and determine the next course of treatment.
I advised the patient to have her family members, including her wife and children, screened for hepatitis C.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen in three weeks to review the results of the additional testing and determine the next course of treatment.
